segments.s472-g49: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when" placed at the clause head. رومی is the subject ("a Roman/the Roman"). به نزد سکندر is a prepositional phrase meaning "to the presence of Alexander" (نزد used with به). رسید is a simple past 3rd-person verb "arrived/reached", completing the clause.
segments.s472-g50: همه is the object meaning "all/everything." یاد کرد is a compound verbal expression (یاد + کردن) meaning "recounted/mentioned"; here کرد is the past tense. آنچ is a relative expression "that which/what," and دید و شنید are coordinated past verbs "saw and heard," forming the content of what was recounted.
segments.s472-g51: وزان is an archaic compound meaning roughly "and from that/about that," linking back to what was just described. تخت و آیین و آن بارگاه is a coordinated list of nouns ("throne and ceremony/customs and that palace"); آن directly modifies بارگاه but the phrase as a whole names the things being spoken of.
segments.s472-g52: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past ("you said"); که introduces a reported-content clause. زنده‌ست is a present/stative predicate "is alive/exists" (زنده + است contracted with the enclitic -ست). بر گاه شاه is a prepositional/time phrase "in the king's time/at the king's court," with گاه meaning "time/occasion" and شاه "king."
segments.s472-g53: سکندر is the subject. ز (poetic) means "from/because of" and takes گفتار او ("his speech/words") as its object; گشت is the past of شدن ("became") and shad is the predicative adjective "happy/joyful," so the clause reads "Alexander, because of his speech, became glad."
segments.s472-g54: به آرام is an adverbial phrase "calmly/quietly" (به + adjective forming an adverbial). تاج کیی is the noun phrase "the Kayanian crown" (کیی = Kayanian). بر نهاد is the verbal phrase: نهاد is the past of نهادن "placed/put," with the preposition بر "on/upon," so together "placed (it) on (his head)." Word order is adverbial → object → particle → verb, typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s472-g1: دلارای is the proper name (Dārā) and is the subject; چون ... شنید forms a temporal clause meaning 'when he heard'. آن سخنها is the direct object 'those words' placed before the verb (typical in older/poetic Persian) and شنید is simple past 'he heard'.
segments.s472-g2: یکی باد سرد = 'a certain/cold wind' (یکی as an indefinite/article-like word). از جگر is a source phrase 'from (his) liver/heart' (جگر used poetically for heart), and برکشید is simple past 3rd-sg 'rose/lifted up'. The whole clause describes a sudden chill rising within him.
segments.s472-g3: ز دارا ز دیده is a doubled prepositional source ('from Dārā, from the eyes')—a poetic way to mark origin/possession. ببارید is past tense 'fell/was raining' (used of drops), and خون is the theme 'blood' (the thing that fell).
segments.s472-g4: که introduces an explanatory/cause clause ('for/because'), بد ریخته is a perfective/past participle phrase meaning 'badly spilled/poured', and زیر خاک اندرون is a locative phrase 'under the inner earth' (زیر = beneath, اندرون = within). The clause explains why blood was falling from the eyes.
segments.s472-g5: نویسنده is the agent 'the scribe' (here likely the one who performed the action); نامه را marks the definite object with را, and پیش خواند is a compound meaning 'read aloud/in the presence of' (پیش = before/in the presence, خواند = read).
segments.s472-g6: همه خون ز مژگان = 'all the blood from (his) eyelashes' (ز indicates source). به رخ برفشاند is a verbal clause 'he splattered/shot onto the face' where به رخ gives direction 'upon the cheek/face' and برفشاند is past 'spattered'. Subject is understood from context (he/Dārā).
segments.s472-g7: مر is an archaic dative 'to me' placed before the object; آن نامه را is 'that letter' with the object marker را, خوب modifies the following noun as 'a good/well (reply)', and پاسخ نوشت is 'wrote a reply' (نوشت = wrote, past 3rd-sg).
segments.s472-g8: سخنهای (plural 'words/speeches') is object, with با مغز و فرخ a descriptive phrase 'with brain/substance and with felicity/auspiciousness' (مغز = sense/substance, فرخ = fortunate/auspicious). نوشت repeats the verb 'wrote' and ties this second object to the same agent—stylistic parallelism.
segments.s472-g9: نخست is an adverb meaning ‘first’/‘at first’ that sets temporal order. آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (an acclamation/praise) + کرد (past tense of کردن ‘to do/make’), here meaning ‘praised’. بر + کردگار uses بر as a preposition/compliment marker meaning ‘to/unto’ (praising directed toward). The subject is omitted (understood from the context) and the verb کرد carries the past tense.
segments.s472-g10: This segment is a string of epithets in apposition to کردگار. The nouns are nominative/appositive titles (جهاندار, دادار, پروردگار) that further identify the one being praised; no additional verb is needed because they attach to the preceding آفرین کرد. The order (noun after noun) is typical of poetic accumulation.
segments.s472-g11: دگر functions as ‘again/then’ marking a new utterance; گفت is the verb ‘said’ (the subject is omitted). کز begins a subordinate clause (relative/causal particle ‘that/which’). کار گردان سپهر is a noun phrase where کار ‘work/operation’ is qualified by گردان ‘revolving’ and followed by سپهر ‘sky/heaven’ — together ‘the workings of the revolving sky’. Word order is verb-final for the subordinate clause because of the poetic style.
segments.s472-g12: کزویست is a fused expression meaning roughly ‘that is from him/it’, functioning to assert origin (‘comes from it’). Then follows a coordinate list of abstract nouns (پرخاش و آرام و مهر) joined by و; these are the things said to issue from the sky’s workings. The whole clause thus states source (کزویست) + list of effects.
segments.s472-g13: همی is the archaic continuous/intensive particle (here ‘always/still’). فر دارا is a noun + adjective/epithet: فر ‘(royal) glory’ qualified by دارا ‘possessor/holding’ (i.e. ‘possessed of glory’). همی ... خواستیم places the habitual particle before the past-tense verb خواستیم (‘we wished/desired’), a common poetic pattern: an iterative or emphatic past (‘we desired repeatedly/always’). The subject is encoded in the verb ending -یم (we).
segments.s472-g14: زبان را uses را as the definite/object marker marking زبان (‘the tongue/speech’) as the direct object. به نام وی is a prepositional phrase ‘with/for his name’ (به + نام + possessive وی). آراستیم is past tense ‘we adorned/embellished’ and agrees with the plural subject (—یم). The clause literally: ‘we adorned the tongue with his name’ = ‘we graced our speech with his name.’
segments.s472-g15: کنون ‘now’ opens the temporal frame. چون here functions as a subordinating conjunction ‘when/as’ (classical use: چون ... گذشت = when ... passed). زمان وی is the noun phrase ‘his time’ (زمان + possessive وی). اندر گذشت is the past verb phrase ‘passed away’ with اندر (in/within) prefixed for poetic emphasis; گذشت is past tense (‘it passed’), so the clause means ‘when his time passed.’
segments.s472-g16: This clause states the result: سر گاه او here functions adverbially/locally (‘at that moment’ or ‘then his head’ depending on reading) where سر ‘head’ and گاه ‘time/occasion’ cluster with possessive او. چوب تابوت is a genitive-like apposition ‘the wood of the coffin’ (چوب = wood, تابوت = coffin). گشت is the past-tense of گشتن used as ‘became/turned into’. Poetic word order allows the copular/resultative verb to follow the affected noun phrase.
segments.s472-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. شاه زمانه is a genitive/compound noun 'the king of the age' (literal word order: noun + modifier). ترا is the enclitic object 'you' attached after the noun phrase; برگزید is the simple past 'chose/selected' placed at the end in the typical SOV order of classical Persian.
segments.s472-g42: سر از رای او is a prepositional-like phrase literally 'head from his opinion/decree' where سر از X idiomatically means to depart or lift oneself from X; کس is the indefinite subject 'no one' (determined by the negative that follows). نیارد is an archaic negative/modal form here carrying the sense 'would not / does not dare' and کشید is the verbal image 'to draw/lift' (as in سر کشیدن = to raise one's head). Together the clause means 'no one would raise their head from his counsel' i.e. 'no one disobeyed/dared oppose him.'
segments.s472-g43: نوشتیم is past tense first-person plural 'we wrote'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. سوی functions as 'to/toward' marking the recipient phrase, and مهتران is the plural 'chiefs/elders' (here the intended meaning is 'leaders'); word order is typical: verb (نوشتیم) then object then directional phrase.
segments.s472-g44: به is the preposition 'to/for'. پهلو here means 'side' or 'flank' and idiomatically 'the ranks/party' (i.e. beside/among). نژادان is the plural 'those of noble lineage' (the born/lineage), and جنگاوران is 'warriors'; the whole phrase specifies the addressees 'to the ranks/party of the noble-born warriors.'
segments.s472-g45: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the clause. فرمان داراست is a verbless/nominal predicate meaning 'possesses command' or 'has authority' (دارا + ست functions as 'possesses / it is in possession'). The second فرمان تو reiterates/specifies whose command it is ('your command') for emphasis; the order is emphatic repetition typical of epic diction.
segments.s472-g46: نپیچد is the negative form of پیچیدن 'to turn/swerve', here meaning 'will not turn/veer away' (the initial ن- negates the verb). کسی in this negative context functions as 'no one'. سر ز پیمان تو literally 'head from your covenant' (سر ز X = to depart from X); together the clause says 'no one will turn away from your covenant/pledge.' Word order places the negative verb first, a common classical/poetic option.
segments.s472-g47: فرستاده را marks 'the messenger' with the definite object marker را (object-focused). جامه و بدره داد is a simple past clause: جامه 'robe' and بدره (a poetic epithet, literally 'full moon', here used as a term for a splendid/beautiful ornament or robe) + داد 'gave'. The verb داد (gave) closes the clause in normal SOV order for Persian epic style.
segments.s472-g48: ز is the classical/prepositional 'from'. گنجش (from lemma گنج) means 'treasury/treasure'. The second ز is a poetic repetition 'from' or 'out of', هرگونه‌ای means 'of every kind', بهره is 'share/portion', and داد repeats the past 'gave'. Together: 'From the treasury he gave a share of every kind.'
segments.s472-g33: مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning "may it not be / lest" and sets up the whole clause as a negative wish. به گیتی is a locative phrase "in the world" (به + گیتی) with slightly archaic/poetic prepositional use of به. به جز کام تو is an exception phrase "except for your desire" (به جز + noun), so the segment as a whole: "May it not be in the world, except for your wish..."
segments.s472-g34: همیشه is an adverb "always." بر ایوانها is a locative prepositional phrase "on/upon the porticoes" (بر + ایوانها). نام تو is the nominal phrase "your name"; the verb is omitted (poetic ellipsis of بودن/باشد or a construction controlled by مبادا), so the full idea reads roughly "that your name be always upon the porticoes."
segments.s472-g35: دگر here is temporal/adverbial "then/again" (a poetic marker of sequence). آنک is the relative/archaic "that/who/which" introducing the clause. از روشنک یاد کرد is a verb phrase "remembered Roshanak" (from/of Roshanak + یاد + کرد), so the segment means "then he who remembered Roshanak..."
segments.s472-g36: دل ما is the noun phrase "our heart(s)." بدان آرزو is a prepositional/benefactive phrase meaning "with that hope" — here بد( = به + آن) is a poetic contraction meaning "to/with that." شاد کرد is a past transitive verb "made (happy) / gladdened." The whole segment reads "he gladdened our heart with that hope." (Note: دان is here functioning as the demonstrative آن in the fixed poetic form بدان.)
segments.s472-g37: پرستنده تست is a short possessive/relational phrase: پرستنده "worshipper/devotee" followed by the archaic clitic/test/word تست (here functioning as an old possessive/relational marker meaning roughly "yours" / "of you"). ما بنده‌ایم is the main clause "we are servants/servile" (بنده‌ایم = we are servants). Together: "(We are) your worshippers — we are servants."
segments.s472-g38: به فرمان و رایت is an instrumental/agentive prepositional phrase "by/through command and (your) standard/banner" (به here = by/through). سرافگنده‌ایم is a predicate in the present indicative "we are ashamed/abashed." Grammatically this gives a predication about the subject (ما): "by your command and banner we are abashed."
segments.s472-g39: درودت (درود + ـت) is "your greeting" (درود with the possessive clitic). فرستاد is a past tense verb "he sent." و links actions; پاسخ نوشت is another past action "wrote an answer." The segment is a simple sequence of two past verbs: "he sent your greeting and wrote an answer."
segments.s472-g40: یکی here is an indefinite marker "a / a certain." خوب پاسخ is "a good/fine answer" (adjective before noun in poetic ordering). بسان بهشت is a simile "like heaven/paradise" (بسان = like/as). Together: "a fine answer like paradise."
segments.s472-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause here = 'when'. خون خداوند is a genitive phrase 'the blood of the lord/sovereign'. ریزد is the classical/past-present form of ریختن used here to mean 'is poured/flows/is shed'; the verb appears inside the temporal clause. کسی stands as an indefinite/relative element 'anyone' that links into the main clause that follows.
segments.s472-g26: به گیتی = a prepositional phrase 'in/throughout the world' (به + گیتی). درنگش is درنگ + -ش (third‑person possessive) meaning 'his/its delay' or 'delay of it'. نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن used impersonally here ('there is not / there will not be'); بسی at the end is an adverbial intensifier 'much/very' (emphasizing that the delay will not be great). Together: 'there will be no great delay in the world.'
segments.s472-g27: دگر here functions adversatively/contrastively ('as for the other/that one' or 'but as for'). آنک is a relative pronoun 'the one who/whom'. جستی is second‑person past (you sought); همی is the archaic verbal particle indicating continued or habitual action or emphasis (often glossed as 'indeed/so'); آشتی is the noun 'reconciliation/peace' and is the object of the verb phrase 'جستی ... آشتی' = 'you sought (and) reconciled (him)'.
segments.s472-g28: بسی روز is a quantifier + noun: 'many/much time' = 'many days'. با پند is a prepositional phrase 'with counsel/advice'. بگذاشتی is the second‑person past of گذاشتن with the prefix ب- (classical form) meaning 'you left/you permitted/you let (him be)'; here it means you left him in that reconciled state for many days, accompanied by counsel.
segments.s472-g29: نیاید is an archaic negative verbal form meaning 'does not come / does not arise'. ز شاهان is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from kings'. پرستندگی is a deverbal noun 'worship/devotion' and here is the semantic subject: 'worship does not come from kings' (i.e. one should not expect worship from kings).
segments.s472-g30: The verb نجوید (classical present) means 'seeks/asks for' or 'desires'. کس here functions with negative sense in the couplet context ('no one'); از marks source/agent 'from'. تاجور is 'merchant/trader', and بندگی is 'servitude/serving' (i.e. being a servant). The clause says that nobody desires servitude from a merchant.
segments.s472-g31: به جای is an idiom meaning 'in the place of / instead of'. شهنشاه is 'the emperor/sovereign' (object of جای). ما را uses the object/topic marker را attached to ما ('us'), so 'you put us' or 'you set us' is implied; توی is an archaic locative 'in/among'. The clause therefore says 'you placed us in the emperor's stead/seat.'
segments.s472-g32: چو = 'when'. خورشید شد is 'the sun became/appeared' (شد here = 'rose/appeared/became'). ماه ما را is 'our moon' with را marking the phrase for emphasis/object focus (classical ordering places possessed noun before the marker). توی again is locative 'in/there'. Taken together: 'When the sun rose, you set our moon there (as well).' The clause parallels the first: placement in exalted positions compared to sun and moon imagery.
segments.s472-g17: ترا (ت + را) is the accusative clitic 'you' (object marker) placed before the verb, so the clause begins with the direct object. خواهم is the 1st-person present/future-form of خواستن ('I will / I desire'); here it expresses the speaker's promise or intention toward the addressee. اندر جهان نیکوی is a locative/adverbial phrase ('in the world, good/excellent') modifying the result the speaker intends to bring about.
segments.s472-g18: This is a simple coordination of three nominal results introduced without an extra verb: بزرگی (greatness), پیروزی (victory) and خسروی (royalness/kingship). The repeated و is the conjunction 'and' linking the nouns; the whole string is the complement/goal of خواهم in the previous segment (i.e. what the speaker will give/grant).
segments.s472-g19: به کام تو خواهم is a fixed phrase 'I will (act) to your pleasure/for your satisfaction' (به = 'to/for', کام = 'wish/desire', تو = 'you', خواهم = 'I will/I wish'). The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause of result or purpose; باشد is the 3rd-person subjunctive/present of بودن ('be'), used here to express the desired/resulting state ('that the world be ...'). جهان is the subject of that subjunctive clause ('the world').
segments.s472-g20: برین آشکارا functions adverbially: برین (بر + این, a poetic/archaic locative sense 'on/over this' or 'before this') together with آشکارا ('openly') describes how the world is to be ('openly, before all'). ندارم نهان is a present negative clause: ندارم = 'I do not have/possess' and نهان = 'hidden' (predicate). In classical Persian the copula is often omitted, so 'ندارم نهان' literally 'I have not (anything) hidden' = 'I hide nothing / I have nothing concealed.'
segments.s472-g21: شنیدم is the 1st-person past ('I heard'). The following nominal group functions as the object of شنیدم: همه هرچ (all / whatever) + گفتی ('that you said' — here used as 'what you said' or 'your words'). ز مهر ('from/with affection') indicates the attitude/source of those words. Word order is verb-first (classical narration) followed by the object phrase.
segments.s472-g22: که introduces a new clause expressing a wish; از جان تو ('because of / on account of your soul' — here 'for your soul') is a prepositional phrase marking the beneficiary or reason. شاد بادا is an optative formula: شاد = 'happy', بادا = 'may it be' (a classical wish particle), so together 'may (it) be happy'. سپهر ('the sky/heavens') is the subject placed at the end for poetic effect: 'may the heavens be glad for your soul.'
segments.s472-g23: ازان is the prepositional phrase 'from that' (از + آن), introducing sources or origins. It governs a coordinated list: دخمه ('tomb/sepulchre') و دار ('and house/abode'). وز here is a poetic contraction of و ز ('and from'), introducing ماهیار (a proper name/epithet 'Mahyār'). The whole phrase lists the places/sources 'from which' the following retribution is to come.
segments.s472-g24: مکافات is a noun 'retribution/recompense' (here the thing wished upon someone). بدخواه 'ill-wisher' modifies/identifies the target, and جانوشیار is a proper name (epithet) in apposition: 'the ill-wisher Jānūshyār.' Classical Persian often omits the copula or verb of wishing in short clauses like this; the sense is optative/imperative (let retribution befall ...).

sentences.s472-s25: When a Roman came before Alexander, he recounted everything he had seen and heard.
sentences.s472-s26: And of that throne, the ceremonies, and that palace you said that they still exist in the king's court.
sentences.s472-s27: Alexander, made glad by his words, calmly placed the Kayanian crown upon (himself).
sentences.s472-s1: When Dārā heard those words, a cold wind rose from his heart.
sentences.s472-s2: Blood poured from Dārā's eyes, for it had been badly spilled beneath the inner earth.
sentences.s472-s3: The scribe read the letter before him; all the blood from his eyelashes spattered upon his face.
sentences.s472-s4: He wrote that letter's reply to me well; he wrote words full of sense and good fortune.
sentences.s472-s5: First he praised the Creator — the Lord of the world, the Giver, the Sustainer.
sentences.s472-s6: Again he said that from the workings of the revolving sky there come contention, calm, and love.
sentences.s472-s7: We have always desired (the) glorious farr; we adorned our speech with his name.
sentences.s472-s8: Now, when his time had passed, at that moment his head became the wood of a coffin.
sentences.s472-s21: When the king of the age chose you, no one would lift his head away from his counsel (i.e. no one would disobey his decree).
sentences.s472-s22: We wrote a letter to the chiefs and to the ranks of the noble-born warriors.
sentences.s472-s23: That your command has authority; no one will turn away from your covenant.
sentences.s472-s24: He gave the messenger robes and a moonlike (splendid) garment; from the treasury he gave a share of every kind.
sentences.s472-s17: May it not be in the world — except for your desire — that your name be always on the porticoes.
sentences.s472-s18: Then he who remembered Roshanak made our heart glad with that hope.
sentences.s472-s19: We are your worshippers — we are servants; by your command and standard we are abashed.
sentences.s472-s20: He sent your greeting and wrote a reply — a fine answer like paradise.
sentences.s472-s13: When the sovereign's blood is shed, no one in the world will delay (matters) long.
sentences.s472-s14: As for the one you sought, you made peace with him, and you left him for many days with counsel.
sentences.s472-s15: Worship does not come from kings; no one seeks servitude from a merchant.
sentences.s472-s16: You set us in the place of the emperor; when the sun rose, you set our moon there as well.
sentences.s472-s9: I will grant you in the world fair renown — greatness, victory, and kingship.
sentences.s472-s10: To please you I will act so that the world be openly thus — I have nothing hidden.
sentences.s472-s11: I heard all that you said out of affection; may the heavens be glad for your soul.
sentences.s472-s12: From that tomb and dwelling and from Mahyār, let retribution come upon the ill-wisher Jānūshyār.
